subject: ap-precalculus
title: Logarithmic Modeling: Video Game Sales & Average Rate of Change
stimulus: On the initial day of sales ($t = 0$) for a new video game, there were 40 thousand units of the game sold that day. Ninety-one days later ($t = 91$), there were 76 thousand units sold that day. The number of units sold on a given day can be modeled by the function $G$ given by $G(t) = a + b\ln(t + 1)$, where $G(t)$ is the number of units sold, in thousands, on day $t$ since the initial day of sales.

parts.prompt:
(i) Use the given data to write two equations that can be used to find the values for constants $a$ and $b$ in the expression for $G(t)$.

(ii) Find the values for $a$ and $b$ as decimal approximations.

scoring_points.description: Two equations involving $a$ and $b$ using the given input-output pairs: $a + b\ln(0 + 1) = 40$ and $a + b\ln(91 + 1) = 76$
wrong_examples: Using incorrect input values such as $G(90)$ instead of $G(91)$
common_traps: Forgetting that $t + 1$ appears in the argument (so at $t=91$, the argument is $\ln(92)$, not $\ln(91)$)

scoring_points.description: Correct values $a = 40$ and $b = 7.961$ (or $b = 7.961451\ldots$); may be presented as standalone values or in an expression for $G(t)$
common_traps: Decimal presentation error: must give at least three decimal places
If the thousands translation is used consistently ($a = 40{,}000$, $b = 7961$), both points are eligible

parts.scoring_notes: The first point is earned for two equations involving $a$ and $b$ using the given input-output pairs. The second point is earned with or without supporting work if correct values are identified. Partial credit (scored 1–0) if one correct equation is present in a set of two AND one correct value is identified.

parts.prompt:
(i) Use the given data to find the average rate of change of the number of units of the video game sold, in thousands per day, from $t = 0$ to $t = 91$ days. Express your answer as a decimal approximation. Show the computations that lead to your answer.

(ii) Use the average rate of change found in (i) to estimate the number of units of the video game sold, in thousands, on day $t = 50$. Show the work that leads to your answer.

(iii) Let $A_t$ represent the estimate of the number of units sold, in thousands, using the average rate of change found in (i). For $A_{50}$ found in (ii), it can be shown that $A_{50} < G(50)$. Explain why, in general, $A_t < G(t)$ for all $t$ where $0 < t < 91$.

scoring_points.description: Correct average rate of change $\frac{G(91) - G(0)}{91 - 0} = \frac{76 - 40}{91} \approx 0.396$ (or $0.395$) thousand units per day, in the presence of a quotient using the given data values
common_traps: Supporting work showing the quotient is required
Units are not needed and are ignored if presented

scoring_points.description: Correct estimate for $t = 50$: $y = 40 + 0.395604 \cdot (50 - 0) \approx 59.780$ thousand, in the presence of supporting work (accept range $[59.750, 59.805]$)
wrong_examples: Estimate of $59.8$ or $60$ without supporting work showing the decimal approximation
common_traps: Final answer may be reported as 59 or 60 thousand provided supporting work shows a correct decimal approximation
If rate in (i) has a decimal presentation error, the consistent value from (i) can earn this point

scoring_points.description: Explanation must include (a) the graph of $G$ is concave down on $(0, 91)$ OR the rate of change of $G$ is decreasing, AND (b) reference to the secant line on $0 < t < 91$ being below the graph of $G$

required_elements: $G$ is concave down OR rate of change of $G$ is decreasing
secant line lies below graph of $G$ on $(0, 91)$
alternatives.correct_example: The estimate $A_t$ is the $y$-coordinate of a point on the secant line through $(0, G(0))$ and $(91, G(91))$. Because the graph of $G$ is concave down on $(0, 91)$, this secant line is below the graph of $G$ on $(0, 91)$. Therefore $A_t < G(t)$ for all $t$ in $(0, 91)$.

wrong_examples: Saying only 'G is increasing' without addressing concavity or secant line placement
Saying only 'the secant line is below the curve' without explaining why (concavity)
common_traps: Both elements — concavity/decreasing rate AND secant line reference — are required to earn this point

parts.scoring_notes: Supporting work is required in (i) and (ii). Units are not needed in (i). The final answer in (ii) may be reported as 59 thousand or 60 thousand if supporting work shows a correct decimal approximation. For (iii), both the concavity/rate-of-change statement AND the secant line reference are required.

parts.prompt: The makers of the video game reported that daily sales of the video game decreased each day after $t = 91$. Explain why the error in the model $G$ increases after $t = 91$.

scoring_points.description: Explanation must connect that the function model $G$ is increasing for $t > 91$ while actual daily sales are decreasing, so the absolute difference between actual and predicted values grows each day after $t = 91$

required_elements: actual daily sales are decreasing for $t > 91$
alternatives.correct_example: For $t > 91$, daily sales are decreasing and $G$ is increasing. Therefore, the absolute value of the difference between actual daily sales and the daily sales predicted by $G$ is increasing each day for $t > 91$.

wrong_examples: Saying only '$G$ keeps increasing' without connecting to the decreasing actual sales
Saying only 'sales decrease' without noting that $G$ predicts the opposite direction
common_traps: Must include an implicit or explicit connection between 'function model is increasing' and 'daily sales are decreasing'

parts.scoring_notes: To earn the point, the reasoning must include an implicit or explicit connection between the 'function model is increasing' and 'daily sales are decreasing.' On day $t = 91$, actual sales and $G(91)$ agree; for $t > 91$ the gap widens because $G$ continues to increase while actual sales fall.
